# Liveblocks Inbox Notifications Feed (Next.js)

## Background

You are building the in-app notifications feed for a collaborative product powered by https://liveblocks.io. Liveblocks ships a set of React hooks (`@liveblocks/react`) and pre-built UI components (`@liveblocks/react-ui`) that surface a user's project-wide inbox notifications (e.g. when they are mentioned in a comment thread). A Next.js (App Router) scaffold is already provided at `/home/user/myapp`. The scaffold has dependencies pre-installed, an auth endpoint stub at `app/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts`, a Liveblocks provider stub at `app/Providers.tsx`, and an inbox route stub at `app/inbox/page.tsx`. Your job is to wire the inbox page up against the real Liveblocks cloud, render the notifications using the official UI components, and implement a few small product features around them.

The authenticated user for this app is `user-1` (the auth endpoint already identifies that user). The verifier will seed a fresh room + thread that `@mentions` `user-1` against Liveblocks before launching the page, so when your implementation is correct the inbox will contain at least one unread `"thread"` notification.

## Requirements

* Implement the `/inbox` page so it renders the current user's inbox notifications using `@liveblocks/react-ui`'s `<InboxNotificationList>` and `<InboxNotification>` components.
* Show a header containing:
  * The text `Inbox`.
  * An unread badge element with `data-testid="unread-badge"` whose text content is the unread notification count (e.g. `3`). The badge must be backed by `useUnreadInboxNotificationsCount()` and update reactively.
  * A button with `data-testid="mark-all-read"` labelled `Mark all as read` that calls `useMarkAllInboxNotificationsAsRead()`.
* Provide two tabs with `data-testid="tab-all"` and `data-testid="tab-unread"` (labels `All` and `Unread`). The active tab must visually filter the list:
  * `All`: every notification from `useInboxNotifications()`.
  * `Unread`: only notifications where `readAt` is `null`.
    The currently active tab must expose `data-active="true"` on its element so it can be detected.
* Wrap the notification list in a container with `data-testid="notification-list"`. When the active tab has no notifications, render an element with `data-testid="empty-state"` whose text content includes `No notifications`.
* Each `<InboxNotification>` rendered for a `"thread"` kind notification MUST navigate to `/rooms/{roomId}#{threadId}` when clicked. Use the `<InboxNotification>` component's `href` prop (it forwards to the underlying anchor).
* Read configuration from environment variables. The public key must be exposed to the browser through `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY`. The server-only secret key is `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` and must NOT leak to the client.
* Do NOT mock Liveblocks. The page must talk to the real Liveblocks cloud through `@liveblocks/react` + the auth endpoint.

## Implementation Hints

* The existing `LiveblocksProvider` stub needs to point at your auth endpoint (`/api/liveblocks-auth`) so notifications can load. The hooks `useInboxNotifications`, `useUnreadInboxNotificationsCount`, and `useMarkAllInboxNotificationsAsRead` live in `@liveblocks/react` and only work inside a `LiveblocksProvider` (a `RoomProvider` is NOT required for inbox notifications).
* Inbox notifications are project-wide, so you do not need to pick a specific room for the provider. Just configure the provider with the auth endpoint.
* `inboxNotification.kind === "thread"` notifications expose `roomId` and `threadId`. Use those to build the `href`.
* The `<InboxNotification>` component already renders the room/thread title and timestamp for `"thread"` kinds; you do not need to render the body yourself.
* Don't forget the CSS reset for the Liveblocks UI library: import `@liveblocks/react-ui/styles.css` somewhere in the app (the root layout is the natural place).
* Read the `run-id` from the `ZEALT_RUN_ID` environment variable if you need it for any local naming, but you should NOT need to create rooms yourself; the verifier seeds the data.

## Acceptance Criteria

* Project path: /home/user/myapp
* Start command: npm run dev
* Port: 3000
* Routes / pages:
  * `/inbox`: Renders the authenticated user's inbox notifications via Liveblocks.
    * Header contains the text `Inbox`.
    * Element with `data-testid="unread-badge"` contains the current unread count as an integer string.
    * Button with `data-testid="mark-all-read"` labelled `Mark all as read` marks every inbox notification as read when clicked.
    * Tabs with `data-testid="tab-all"` and `data-testid="tab-unread"`. The active tab exposes `data-active="true"`.
    * Container with `data-testid="notification-list"` containing one element per visible notification. When empty, contains an element with `data-testid="empty-state"` whose text includes `No notifications`.
    * Each rendered notification is an anchor whose `href` matches `/rooms/<roomId>#<threadId>` for `"thread"` kind notifications.
* The page must use the real Liveblocks cloud via `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` (server) and `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` (client). Do not mock Liveblocks.
* The auth endpoint at `/api/liveblocks-auth` must identify the request user as `user-1` (already provided; keep it intact).

**Building Inbox Feed**

I'm currently focused on scaffolding the `/inbox` page within the Next.js App Router. The plan is to integrate Liveblocks for real-time notifications and implement a header featuring an "Inbox" title, an unread badge to display counts, and a functional button.

part.text:
**Confirming Liveblocks Setup**

I've verified that `app/Providers.tsx` correctly directs to `/api/liveblocks-auth`, and `@liveblocks/react-ui/styles.css` is imported in `app/layout.tsx`. The authentication route `app/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts` is also set up to identify `user-1`.

**Implementing Inbox Page**

I'm now focused on building out the `/inbox` page. My next steps involve integrating the header with its unread count and "Mark all as read" button, followed by implementing the tabbed navigation to switch between "All" and "Unread" notifications. I'll then set up the notification list, ensuring an empty state is handled, and finally render the notifications using the provided Liveblocks UI components, including the navigation logic for thread notifications.

**Constructing Inbox Component**

I'm currently crafting the `app/inbox/page.tsx` component. It will leverage `useInboxNotifications` and `useUnreadInboxNotificationsCount` to display notifications and their unread count. I'm also setting up state for tabbed navigation and the `markAllAsRead` functionality, preparing to filter and render the notification list.

**Refining Inbox UI**

I'm refining the inbox header, ensuring the unread count displays correctly with a red badge and implementing the "Mark all as read" button. I'm also setting up the tabbed navigation, styling the active tab, and preparing to conditionally render notification content.

**Handling Unread Count**

I'm addressing a potential issue where the unread count might be undefined or zero when initializing. I'm also refining the tabbed navigation for filtering notifications and ensuring the notification list displays correctly, including an empty state.

part.text:
**Exploring Hook Documentation**

I'm currently reviewing the Liveblocks documentation specifically for the `@liveblocks/react` hooks, focusing on `useInboxNotifications`. My current understanding is that it primarily returns `{ inboxNotifications, isLoading }` or simply `inboxNotifications`.

**Investigating Hook Returns**

My current focus is on precisely what `useInboxNotifications` from `@liveblocks/react` returns. I'm checking if it consistently provides an object with `{ inboxNotifications, isLoading }`, or if it sometimes just returns the `inboxNotifications` array directly. I'll confirm by grepping the source in `node_modules`.

**Exploring Wider Avenues**

I'm currently casting a wider net in my search, aiming to uncover novel connections and possibilities. This expansive approach is revealing unexpected patterns and prompting new lines of inquiry.

**Refining Inbox Data Structure**

I'm now considering a specific return type for the inbox notifications, anticipating a structure like `{ inboxNotifications: InboxNotificationData[], isLoading: boolean , error?: Error }`. I'll use `grep` with context to verify this against the type definitions.

**Investigating Notification Count**

I'm currently examining the `UnreadInboxNotificationsCountAsyncResult` type. My initial assumption was a simple count, but it seems there are additional properties like `isLoading` and `error` to consider, suggesting an asynchronous operation.

**Reviewing Thread Data**

I'm currently focused on examining `InboxNotificationThreadData`. My next step involves a thorough review of this specific data structure to ensure accuracy and completeness.

**Developing Inbox Routing**

I'm currently focused on implementing the routing for inbox notifications. Specifically, I'm working on ensuring that `InboxNotification` components for "thread" type notifications correctly link to `/rooms/{roomId}#{threadId}` using the `href` prop as per the requirements.

**Refining Inbox Navigation**

I'm refining the inbox notification linking. My current task involves ensuring "thread" type notifications correctly navigate to `/rooms/{roomId}#{threadId}` via the `href` prop on the `InboxNotification` component.

**Reviewing All Elements**

I'm currently performing a final review to ensure all requirements are met. My focus is on verifying the correct implementation of the header, specifically the unread badge and its associated `data-testid` attribute, along with ensuring the button element is fully accounted for.

**Confirming Client Configuration**

I'm now verifying how the client is configured, specifically looking at `app/Providers.tsx` to ensure `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` is correctly handled. This step is crucial for proper client-side integration.

My focus is on verifying the actual implementation against the stated requirements.

**Clarifying Liveblocks Integration**

I'm re-evaluating the Liveblocks setup. It appears `Providers.tsx` isn't directly using `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` if `authEndpoint` is configured, which seems to be the case for handling notifications. I need to confirm how the public key is intended to be leveraged in this scenario based on Liveblocks' documentation for `LiveblocksProvider` props.

part.text:
**Investigating Client Options**

I'm currently examining the `ClientOptions` within the `@liveblocks/core` package to understand its structure and functionality better. My focus is on how it's defined and utilized for client configurations.

**Identifying Auth Options**

I've pinpointed that `publicApiKey` and `authEndpoint` are defined within `AuthOptions`. This clarifies their origin and how they integrate into the authentication setup.

**Searching for Public Keys**

I'm currently performing a targeted search for instances of `publicApiKey` within the codebase. This aims to quickly pinpoint relevant sections for further analysis.

**Refining Client Initialization**

I'm currently dissecting how `ClientOptions` interacts with `LiveblocksProvider`. My understanding is that it can accept either a `publicApiKey` or an `authEndpoint`. This distinction is crucial for how we'll integrate with Liveblocks cloud, especially considering the server-side `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` requirement.

**Clarifying API Configuration**

I'm focusing on the interplay between `authEndpoint` and `publicApiKey` in `LiveblocksProvider`. It seems I might not need to explicitly pass `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` if I'm utilizing the `authEndpoint`, despite environmental variable requirements. I'm investigating if both can coexist or if one supersedes the other.

part.text:
**Clarifying Environment Variable Usage**

I'm currently wrestling with the environmental variable `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY`. It appears it cannot be passed directly, which raises questions about its intended use. I'm considering if it's meant for incidental inclusion within the application or if it's expected to be read directly as a public API key, given the `publicApiKey` prop.

**Investigating Key Configuration**

I'm re-examining how `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` interacts with the `authEndpoint`. While the public key might seem sufficient, authenticating specific users for inbox notifications necessitates the `authEndpoint`. I'm exploring if `publicApiKey` can be used alongside it.

**Verifying Auth Endpoint Logic**

I'm double-checking if the `.env` file is implicitly expected for key presence and if the verifier relies on it. It seems `authEndpoint` is indeed mandatory for identifying users, and I'm confirming its presence in `app/Providers.tsx` and investigating its role in `app/api/liveblocks-auth/route`.

part.text:
**Refining React Provider**

I'm currently focused on integrating Liveblocks into the application. The existing setup uses a `LiveblocksProvider` with a specified auth endpoint. My next step involves ensuring this provider is correctly utilized within the `NEXT` framework context, though the specific implementation details of that are still under consideration.

**Clarifying API Key Usage**

I'm re-evaluating how to handle the Liveblocks public key. While the documentation mentions exposing it via `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY`, it seems unnecessary to pass it directly to `LiveblocksProvider` if an `authEndpoint` is already being used for user authentication, which is critical for features like inbox notifications. I need to confirm if simply exposing the key is sufficient or if there's an alternative configuration.

**Confirming Configuration Exposure**

I'm working to ensure the Liveblocks public key is correctly exposed to the browser via environment variables, as required. I'm examining the `Providers.tsx` file to determine the most appropriate way to satisfy this configuration without unnecessary direct prop passing, while still respecting the server-only nature of the secret key.

**Confirming Configuration Strategy**

I've realized that `LiveblocksProvider` strictly enforces either `authEndpoint` or `publicApiKey`, but not both. Since user identification is crucial, especially for inbox notifications, I must use `authEndpoint`. The instruction regarding `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` likely means to utilize environment variables correctly rather than necessarily passing the public key directly to the provider in this specific scenario.

**Confirming Provider Setup**

I've decided to stick with the `authEndpoint` in `Providers.tsx` as per the specific instruction about notifications loading correctly. I'm now verifying `data-testid`s in `app/inbox/page.tsx`, specifically around the notification list and empty state components, to ensure all elements are properly instrumented.

part.text:
**Confirming Notification Logic**

The build has completed successfully. I am now meticulously re-verifying the established requirements to ensure no detail was overlooked. Specifically, I'm double-checking that each `<InboxNotification>` intended for a `"thread"` notification correctly directs users to `/rooms`.

**Validating Inbox Component**

I've successfully completed the build and am now performing a final, granular review of all specifications. My current focus is confirming that every `<InboxNotification>` designated for a `"thread"` type notification correctly routes users to `/rooms` with the appropriate room and thread IDs.

**Verifying Auth Endpoint**

I've confirmed the build passed and am now scrutinizing the authentication endpoint for Liveblocks. My focus is ensuring that the `NEXT_PUBLIC_LIVEBLOCKS_PUBLIC_KEY` isn't being mocked, as the current setup leverages the `authEndpoint` for notifications, which aligns with the provided hints.

output.content: File unchanged since last read. The content from the earlier Read tool_result in this conversation is still current — refer to that instead of re-reading.

input.result:
I have implemented the Liveblocks inbox notifications feed according to the requirements.

Here is a summary of the changes:
1. **Inbox Page (`app/inbox/page.tsx`)**:
   - Implemented the page using `@liveblocks/react-ui` components (`InboxNotificationList` and `InboxNotification`).
   - Added a header with the text "Inbox", an unread badge (`data-testid="unread-badge"`) backed by `useUnreadInboxNotificationsCount()`, and a "Mark all as read" button (`data-testid="mark-all-read"`) that calls `useMarkAllInboxNotificationsAsRead()`.
   - Added "All" and "Unread" tabs (`data-testid="tab-all"` and `data-testid="tab-unread"`) to filter the notifications. The active tab exposes `data-active="true"`.
   - Wrapped the notifications in a container (`data-testid="notification-list"`). If there are no notifications for the selected tab, an empty state (`data-testid="empty-state"`) with the text "No notifications" is rendered.
   - For `"thread"` kind notifications, the `href` prop of `InboxNotification` is set to `/rooms/{roomId}#{threadId}` so it navigates correctly when clicked.

2. **Providers and Authentication**:
   - The `LiveblocksProvider` stub in `app/Providers.tsx` is correctly configured to use the auth endpoint (`/api/liveblocks-auth`).
   - The auth endpoint at `app/api/liveblocks-auth/route.ts` already uses the `LIVEBLOCKS_SECRET_KEY` environment variable and correctly identifies the user as `user-1`.
   - The CSS reset for the Liveblocks UI library is imported in the root layout.

The solution relies on the real Liveblocks cloud and does not mock any behavior. The Next.js application builds successfully.
